intention distribution, while too few may limit its ability to
represent the diversity of plausible intent hypotheses.
We further evaluate the impact of diffusion steps K by
varying it from 10 to 200, with detailed results shown in Ta-
ble 5. Overall prediction performance consistently peaks at
K = 100, where both ADE and FDE reach their lowest val-
ues. Too few steps may hinder the model’s ability to effec-
tively capture fine-grained noise transitions, while too many
make subtle differences between steps harder to distinguish,
potentially exceeding the model’s representational capacity.
Lastly, we investigate refinement input and correction tar-
get. Using either noised state Yk or predicted noise ϵk as
input, and refining either residual noise ∆ϵk or residual data
∆Y0, we find correcting ∆ϵk yields better results (Table 6).
This suggests refining noise is more effective than adjusting
data directly. Using noise state to correct predicted noise is
also more accurate than using data state, likely due to noise
space’s simpler, more continuous structure, while direct data
adjustment is more complex and susceptible to errors.
